title: EB-1B Visa for Professors & Researchers | Colombo & Hurd
content:
## EB-1B Attorneys for Exceptional Professors and Researchers
The EB-1B visa is for distinguished professors and researchers with internationally recognized achievements. If you have a strong record of publications, citations, or groundbreaking work, this visa may be your direct route to a U.S. green card.
At Colombo & Hurd, our EB-1B attorneys craft persuasive petitions, highlight your accomplishments, and secure expert recommendations to present the strongest case to USCIS.
- Over 20 years representing clients worldwide
- Thousands of U.S. green cards approved

## More than 20 years of Excellence in Immigration Law

## What is the EB-1B Visa?
The Employment-Based First Preference Outstanding Professors and Researchers (EB-1B) visa provides a pathway to U.S. permanent residency for academics and researchers who have achieved international recognition in their field. To qualify, petitioners must demonstrate a strong record of scholarly accomplishments, along with at least three years of teaching or research experience. In addition, a permanent job offer is required from a U.S. university, institution of higher education, or a company with substantial research activity.
- Permanent Residency for You and Your Family
- No Work Restrictions
- No Labor Certification (PERM) Delays
- A path to U.S. citizenship

## Do You Qualify For the EB-1B Visa?
To qualify for the EB-1B, you must meet both basic requirements and provide evidence of international recognition in your field.
- Permanent job offer (Must Have)
- Three years of experience (Must Have)

## Evidence of International Recognition
USCIS requires proof in two or more of the following categories. Most successful EB-1B petitions combine several of these criteria, supported by strong recommendation letters and documentation of your impact.
- Major awards or prizes
- Membership in associations
- Published material about you
- Judging the work of others
- Original contributions
- Authorship of scholarly work

## Frequently Asked Questions About the EB-1B Visa
Yes, private companies can sponsor EB-1B petitions if they have a dedicated research department and employ at least three full-time researchers. Many corporate R&D labs qualify.
USCIS looks for evidence that your work is widely respected in your field. This can include peer-reviewed publications, citations by other researchers, invitations to present at international conferences, or major awards.
Yes. Both teaching and research experience can be counted if they were completed at recognized institutions and are properly documented.
Yes. Many EB-1B applicants are already in the U.S. on other visas. You can adjust status if you’re eligible or apply from abroad through consular processing.
Not necessarily. While grant funding strengthens your case, it is not required . Strong publications, citations, and documented impact of your work are often enough.
Processing times vary, but EB-1 petitions generally move faster than other employment-based categories. Premium processing is often available, which can shorten initial USCIS review to 15 days .
Absolutely . Letters from independent expert s in your field are a key part of the most successful EB-1B petitions. Our attorneys help identify the right recommenders and shape persuasive letters.
Yes. Your spouse and unmarried children under 21 may apply for permanent residency as derivative beneficiaries of your EB-1B petition.
You may be able to file an appeal, a motion to reopen, or explore alternative immigration options su ch as EB-2 NIW or O-1 visas. Our attorneys advise the best path forward if this occurs.
No. EB-1A is for individuals with extraordinary ability and allows self-petitioning, while EB-1B is specifically for outstanding professors and researchers and requires a sponsoring employer.
